posts.caption:
Chanel returns to Biarritz for Cruise 2027, where Gabrielle Chanel established her first couture house and redefined modern fashion. “Biarritz plays a fundamental role in the history of Chanel,” said Bruno Pavlovsky, president of fashion at Chanel. “We are delighted that Matthieu Blazy has chosen this destination, so dear to the house, to present his first vision of the cruise collection.” Swipe to discover the story behind Biarritz.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @chanelofficial
#chanel #chanelcruise
posts.alt_text: Photo by A2Z Fashion Magazine on April 28, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says '#FASHION 0 আল BONJOUR BIARRITZ, THE STORY BEHIND CHANEL CRUISE CRUISE2027SHOW 2027 SHOW ΛΖ'.

posts.caption:
Chanel Cruise 2027, presented today in Biarritz, marks Matthieu Blazy’s first collection for the House—and a symbolic return to one of Gabrielle Chanel’s most iconic seaside settings.
There’s a quiet shift in tone here. The familiar Chanel codes are all present—tailoring, nautical references, soft elegance—but they feel eased, loosened, and reworked with a lighter hand. Silhouettes move more freely, textures feel less rigid, and the overall mood leans into something more natural and lived-in.
Early reactions highlight this balance: respect for heritage without feeling trapped by it. Rather than a dramatic reinvention, Blazy seems focused on recalibrating the house language—keeping what is unmistakably Chanel, but letting it breathe a little more.
Set against the coastal backdrop of Biarritz, the collection feels like a conversation between past and present—rooted in history, but looking outward.
P.S. OMG, We need these Chanel hats ASAP!
What’s your favourite look?
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷  Isidore Montag (GoRunway.com)
#Chanel #Cruise2027 #MatthieuBlazy #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
We need a minute for Lady Gaga & Doechii in Runway 😭
The pair just dropped the visual for their new track tied to The Devil Wears Prada 2, and it fully leans into the chaos. Directed by Parris Goebel, it plays out like a nonstop runway sequence—no real pause, just look after look, set after set.
It’s fast, theatrical, and completely over the top. The kind of video that actually feels like a music video again—big energy, real staging, and no holding back.
Good luck picking a favourite. 👠✨
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

posts.caption:
Born in Hawick in 1969, Scottish painter @andrew.cranston came to art circuitously – from a small Borders town, via music and manual work, to the Royal College of Art and, eventually, a studio in Glasgow. ⁠
⁠
His paintings, often made on found book covers, unfold like half-remembered stories, shaped by what he calls “creative misremembering”, where domestic scenes slip gently into the uncanny. At the link in bio, the artist walks @pconsuelabh6 through his remarkable new exhibition at @stuartshavemodernart in London 📲⁠
⁠
📸 I’m going in a field by Andrew Cranston. Courtesy the artist and Modern Art © Andrew Cranston. Photo: Modern Art⁠
1. Practice, 2026⁠
2. No lines allowed, 2026⁠
3. Empty nesting, 2026⁠
4. I thought I saw an eagle, 2026⁠
5. The ghost of Lambie, 2026

posts.caption:
@chadmooreholla_ is best known for his tender, spontaneous pictures of New York’s bright young things, but the American photographer’s latest photo book is a departure from his usual portraits. ⁠
⁠
As the title suggests, Eyes and Skies (published by @superlabo), moves between the micro and the macro as it presents his photographs of sunsets and sunrises, skylines and starry nights alongside his close-up studies of eyes. ⁠
⁠
Moore first turned his lens skyward during Covid, creating his previous book Anybody Anyway – a monograph combining his dreamy portraits with pictures of the cosmos. In a sense, Eyes and Skies picks up where Anybody Anyway left off – contemplating the mysteries of the universe. ⁠
⁠
“I started doing the sky images quite slowly, as I matured with making photos and got bored with waiting around for something to happen. Most of my images rely on someone else – typically my friends,” he tells @ohnoitsemilyd. “I just had the thought, ‘What has the same energy as an emotional portrait of a human subject?’, and I figured the closest thing would be the stars.” Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Eyes and Skies by @chadmooreholla_⁠
⁠
#ChadMoore⁠

posts.caption:
John Waters, the self-styled “Pope of Trash”, turns 80 today. ⁠
⁠
He’s the man who spent his teenage years taking LSD in the suburbs of Baltimore with Divine, who he later directed to eat dog shit in the cult film Pink Flamingoes. He’s also the author of the single best piece of romantic advice ever given: “If you go home with someone and they don’t have any books, don’t fuck them.” So with the latter quip in mind, we looked back on his interview with #CathClarke featured in the S/S14 issue of AnOther Magazine.⁠
⁠
From dealing with bullies to how to stay young, he offers an unconventional guide to life. Read it at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #JohnWaters. Photography by @mauricehaasphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of standing, magazine and text that says 'AnOther John Waters Waters on Staying Young, Regrets and More'.

For Spring 2025 at Shanghai Fashion Week, Mark Gong (@markgong_official) reimagined the runway as Charlotte York’s elegant Upper East Side apartment, with polished tailoring, soft florals, gingham, and classic polka dots woven throughout the collection. The show began with the Lily dress, a cream silhouette marked by hand-beaded handprints, drawing on the ‘Sex and the City 2’ scene where Charlotte’s daughter ruined her vintage Valentino (@maisonvalentino) skirt with paint. The detail added a playful layer of storytelling to the collection.
📸: IMDb and Brand Website

posts.caption:
Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.
Charlee Fraser wears @ralphlauren Collection in the April 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”
Continue reading Charlee Fraser’s interview with BAZAAR at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
More than a century after Gabrielle Chanel established her couture house in Biarritz, the French Maison is returning to the Basque coast for its Cruise 2026/27 collection.
It was here, in 1915, that Gabrielle Chanel first laid the foundations for the House’s now-signature codes of freedom, functionality and pared-back elegance, redefining womenswear through fluid jersey, relaxed tailoring and effortless seaside glamour.
Now, Matthieu Blazy’s first Cruise collection for CHANEL revisits the city’s oceanic spirit and artistic legacy, bringing the House back to the very place where the @chanelofficial silhouette first began to take shape.

posts.caption:
Many in the fashion industry predicted that consumers would come to accept AI imagery as its use in marketing and design became more common, and quality improved. But sentiment has only continued to sour as AI has become an even bigger part of the public discourse.
There are a variety of reasons for these feelings, including AI’s effect on jobs, its impact on the environment, the fact that its training data was used without consent and its potential for concentrating more wealth and power in the hands of tech billionaires.
Fashion and beauty brands hurrying to integrate AI across their businesses can’t afford to dismiss these views; they can have a direct impact on how brands are perceived.
BoF’s Marc Bain (@marcbain_) explores why brands must reckon with these sentiments — or risk being caught in the growing backlash. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
The founder had everything: a Bergdorf window, two seasons of sold-out drops, a customer waiting list and a deck that opened with “the next generation of European luxury.” Six months later, the fund raising process was on hold and the 8th arrondissement bankers had stopped returning calls. Not because the brand was weak, but because every investor who passed on it said the same thing privately: we couldn’t get comfortable with what happens after the hype.
This is not another piece about valuation compression. Founders have heard that speech. What they have not heard, because nobody on either side of the table has an incentive to say it out loud, is something more basic: every person sitting across from you in a capital raise has an agenda. That agenda is legitimate, rational and almost never fully disclosed. Understanding this is not cynicism. It is the single most important thing a founder can do before starting a process.
Olivier Rivard-Cohen explores the hidden dynamics behind investor decisions and what founders need to know—read his full opinion piece at the #linkinbio
📷 @rhode

posts.caption:
The beauty industry has seen extraordinary growth and popularity over the last decade, with a flurry of founder-led and celebrity-backed brands disrupting the category and rattling incumbents. Some of the buzziest brands have been snapped up by conglomerates like L’Oréal and Shiseido.
But how do you extend this buzz for the long haul? Labels that have clear sight of who their customer is, create a unique product assortment and marry it with a distinct visual point of view can still resonate with shoppers for many years.
BoF’s latest case study by senior correspondent and special projects editor Daniela Morosini (@danielakmorosini) explores how the likes of The Ordinary, Touchland, Judydoll and Harry’s have created forever brands in a competitive market.
Download the ”How to Build a Forever Beauty Brand″ case study now at the #linkinbio

posts.caption:
For the global luxury industry, Salone del Mobile in Milan has become a moment where brands look beyond the runway to expand into the broader "lifestyle" economy. At the centre of this intersection is @DimoreStudio, co-founded by Britt Moran (@brittmoranmilano) and @EmilianoSalci — a studio that has defined the aesthetic language for luxury hospitality, retail and private residential projects worldwide.
As luxury conglomerates increasingly pursue the "home" and hospitality categories to drive long-term growth, Britt Moran offers an insider's perspective on why credibility in this space can't be bought — it has to be built.
This week on The BoF Podcast, Moran joins BoF founder @ImranAmed during Milan Design Week to discuss what fashion brands get wrong about design, why physical spaces still win over screens, and how a partnership that began after a romantic relationship ended became one of the most influential studios in the world.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Stefano Galuzzi

posts.caption:
Hi this is @ImranAmed, welcome to my weekly letter.
Each year during Milan Design Week, I make a point of visiting @laboratorioparavicini, a small ceramics atelier. I’ve admired their work for years, but this time I got to meet the people behind it — and it changed how I understood the place entirely.
Costanza Paravicini founded the workshop in the 1990s. More than three decades later, she still runs the company with her daughters Benedetta and Margherita. There is an elegance about these women that is ineffably Italian: You can tell immediately that they are doing this work because they love it.
Sales flow through their own e-commerce site as well as curated platforms. But they can’t really keep up with demand. So, at times, the family turns to technology to help achieve more scale: A handpainted plate costs around €300, while a digitally printed version runs closer to €115.
It is into this more traditional world of craft that technology arrived with considerable ambition at this year’s Design Week.
@Samsung’s first-ever chief design officer Mauro Porcini staged his inaugural Salone installation at Superstudio Più: “Design Is an Act of Love,” an exhibition spanning 12 immersive zones.
We’ve heard that vision before. Remember Clippy, the animated paperclip that popped up unbidden in Microsoft Office? Or Apple’s Siri, which has never quite made the interaction feel natural? But this time there was much more attention paid to the design of the devices themselves, with the goal of giving technology more of a human quality. The exhibition opened with a choreographed dance of Samsung devices that reappeared throughout the rest of the show.
Over at @Nike’s Air_Lab installation, chief design officer Martin Lotti talked me through the brand’s 50-year history of “designing with air.” The brand showcased an inflatable puffer jacket with air bags that can be filled using a small pump, as well as a running shirt engineered with openings that capture air to cool the body — demonstrated by a runner on a treadmill, with heat tracking on a screen showing the technology at work.
Read my full letter on where craft, tech and design meet at Design Week #linkinbio

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Nike faces backlash; Chanel picks Elordi — plus other key industry moves.
1. @Moncler reported a 12 percent sales increase in the first quarter, driven by strong demand in Asia, particularly China and Korea.
2. @Gap’s collaboration with @VictoriaBeckham garnered instant buzz online, with several pieces — which run from $34 to $328 — selling out within a few hours.
3. Laila Gohar (@lailacooks)’s reworked antique merry-go-round for @Arket was among the standout activations from fashion brands at the Salone del Mobile design fair Milan, with other notable installations presented by @Gucci and @LoroPiana.
4. Brands that implemented tariff-induced price hikes, including @ElfCosmetics and @Lululemon, are facing class action lawsuits from consumers hoping to recoup their losses after the US government opened a tariff refund portal for businesses this week.
5. Bernard Arnault reaffirmed his confidence in @LVMH at the company’s annual general meeting this week, even as stagnant sales, a tumbling share price and questions over succession have dented its image.
6. @Chanelofficial named “Euphoria” star Jacob Elordi as the new face of Bleu de Chanel, replacing Timothée Chalamet, who was named as an ambassador in 2023. Elordi’s first campaign will drop in May.
7. Advocacy group The Clean Clothes Campaign and four Dutch consumers are suing @Levis, alleging the company misled consumers on the ethics of its labour practices.
8. @Lululemon dismissed activist investor Elliott International’s wishes to select Ralph Lauren veteran Jane Nielsen as its new CEO, instead picking @Nike executive Heidi O’Neill. The news sent its stock price to a 52-week low.
9. @Nike’s latest attempt to recapture the marketing zeitgeist hit a roadblock after its ad for the Boston Marathon, which read "Runners welcome. Walkers tolerated,” faced backlash for “pace shaming.” The brand quickly removed the ad.
This Week in Fashion is BoF’s weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

posts.caption:
On Iran's islands, an intriguing portrait carries new meaning.
Photographer Hoda Afshar recalls the image as something almost accidental. Working with a medium-format analog camera, she had been adjusting, focusing, waiting. Salimeh stood patiently. Then the wind came and she pressed the shutter.
Afshar has been returning to Iran's southern islands of Hormuz and Qeshm since 2015, photographing the land, its residents and the invisible, esoteric forces that shape life there — the winds, which locals believe to be powerful entities. In Afshar's 2021 portrait, Salimeh's mask, painted with thick eyebrows and a mustache, is part of that belief. It is meant to deceive the spirits, to make her appear male. Women, it is believed, are more vulnerable to harmful winds.
Years later, amid a war between the US, Israel, and Iran, these islands in the Strait of Hormuz are caught in a different kind of bluster. Warships, an ongoing US blockade, and Iranian sea mines now threaten the waters around Hormuz and Qeshm, placing communities long shaped by trade and migration at the center of a global crisis.
Afshar's family still lives on Qeshm. From afar, she hears fragments of what daily life has become: the heavy military presence, the bombings that, as she said one relative put it, "cut through your body like an earthquake."
Her images feel newly charged in this context, poetic portraits unfolding in a landscape now threatened by war.
Tap the link in @cnn bio for more.
📸 : Hoda Afshar

posts.caption:
Curator Jeffrey Uslip wants everyone to know that everything is normal.
"This is the smoothest exhibition I've curated in 30 years," said Uslip, who has been working on the US Pavilion at the @venice.art.biennale 2026, which opens in two weeks.
Uslip was insistent on the point. "I also just want to be clear, because I know how people talk, it's important to know that we have had complete artistic autonomy throughout this process," he said by phone. Later, by email, he emphasized the process to select the artist — the sculptor almaallen — was "exactly the same" as it always was.
There's a reason Uslip was adamant. This year's US Pavilion, which is partly funded by the government, has been plagued by controversies and delays. For months, it was uncertain whether the State Department, whose Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs oversees the search, would select an artist to present in Venice at all.
Swipe to read more, and see the full story at link in bio.
📸: File Photo/Elisabetta Villa/Getty Images | Beowulf Sheehan | Ana Hop | Spencer Wells | Ed Mumford

posts.caption:
Ten years ago today, @beyonce released her pioneering visual album "Lemonade," which solidified the singer and songwriter as one of the most creative and audacious pop stars of her generation. The project also inspired countless trends, including the "Lemonade braids" she wore on the album's cover, her first experimentation with country music in "Daddy Lessons" and a banner season for Red Lobster after she name dropped the chain restaurant in "Formation."
One particular look may define the album: the mustard @roberto_cavalli dress Beyoncé wears to take a retaliatory baseball bat to parked cars, a fire hydrant, a piñata, and shop windows in her fevered, plucky ode to her lover's betrayal, "Hold Up." The slightly sheer cascade of ruffles, with a thin lace of straps at the neck, hardly grabbed attention when it debuted in the Fall 2016 Roberto Cavalli show, which was inspired by 1970s rock 'n roll glamour.
Stylist @bcompleted plucked it from countless yellow dresses however, and on a grinning, retaliatory Beyoncé, its louche frills transformed into a statement of feminine power, freedom and vengeance.
📸: @beyonce; @parkwood

posts.caption:
In the 2000s, Chanel created some of the most iconic and luxurious advertising campaigns in fashion history. The brand focused on elegance, glamour, and cinematic storytelling. One of the most famous campaigns was the 2004 Chanel No. 5 advertisement starring Nicole Kidman and directed by Baz Luhrmann. It was produced like a short Hollywood film, featuring romance, mystery, and high fashion. The campaign showed Chanel not just as a perfume brand, but as a symbol of fantasy, sophistication, and timeless beauty.
Throughout the decade, Chanel’s campaigns often featured famous actresses, luxurious couture, and artistic photography. The visual style was elegant, dramatic, and instantly recognizable. Chanel successfully combined classic French luxury with modern celebrity culture, making its campaigns unforgettable and highly influential in the fashion world.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 27, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'From The Archives: Chanel Ad's in the 2000's CULT'.

posts.caption:
The editorial “Horror” by Sofia Sanchez and Mauro Mongiello for Numéro China (December 2015) builds a dense, unsettling visual world that pushes fashion imagery into more psychological territory. Starring Kadri Vahersalu, the series places her in a sequence of staged scenes that deliberately distort conventional ideas of beauty and elegance.
The images feel carefully constructed, but not static. Poses are slightly off, expressions remain restrained, and the settings introduce a quiet sense of unease. What starts as polished quickly shifts into something more ambiguous and harder to read.
Across the series, there’s a clear tension between control and disruption. The fashion stays precise and refined, but it is placed in compositions that feel theatrical and unsettling. Rather than following a narrative, the images work as fragments of mood and atmosphere.
The title “Horror” refers less to a literal story and more to the overall feeling running through the editorial — something subtle, slightly off, and difficult to place.
What stands out most is how fashion is used to shift perception: still rooted in styling and composition, but moving toward something more psychological than purely aesthetic.
⸻
Credits:
Photography: Sofia Sanchez & Mauro Mongiello
Model: Kadri Vahersalu
Styling: Samuel François
Make-up: Lottie
Hair: Romina Manenti
Publication: Numéro China, December 2015
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 26, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'We Deserve More Editorials like this! CULT®'.

posts.caption:
The SS22 women’s collection by Dries Van Noten feels like a direct response to something fashion has been missing for a while: a real, unapologetic relationship with Color ! Instead of treating color as an accent or a detail, it becomes the starting point, something that shapes mood, movement, and atmosphere from the very beginning.
Inspired in part by the energy of festivals and the visual overload of celebrations like Holi, the collection builds looks that feel alive in motion. Prints overlap, tones clash and harmonize at the same time, and textures intensify the effect rather than soften it. Nothing feels neutral or restrained. It’s a collection that leans fully into saturation and contrast, but always with control underneath the surface.
What stands out most is how rare this kind of approach feels right now. So much of contemporary fashion tends to move toward minimal palettes or safe combinations, where color is used carefully, almost cautiously. Here, it’s the opposite. Color is allowed to take space, to interact, to create tension and joy at the same time.
There’s something genuinely refreshing about seeing clothes that don’t try to tone themselves down. The collection suggests that dressing can still be emotional, that putting on color can change how something feels, not just how it looks. It’s not about being loud for the sake of it, but about rediscovering how powerful color can be when it’s used without hesitation.
In that sense, SS22 doesn’t just present clothing, it reaffirms color as something to experience, not just observe !
Credits:
Brand: Dries Van Noten
Collection: Spring/Summer 2022 Womenswear
Designer: Dries Van Noten
Creative direction / runway concept: Dries Van Noten
Photography & film (campaign imagery): Albert Moya, Rafael Pavarotti
Hair: Sam McKnight
Makeup: Lucy Bridge
Collection inspiration reference: Holi festival & festival culture
Season: SS22
Presentation: Digital runway presentation (Paris Fashion Week)

posts.caption:
The editorial moves through a series of characters rather than telling one clear story. Every image feels like a different version of the same idea, shifting between moods, identities, and expressions.
In “Una Canzone” for Vogue Italia (October 2018), Luigi & Iango take inspiration from Mina Mazzini, one of Italy’s most iconic singers. She wasn’t just known for her voice, but for turning her entire image into a form of expression, and that energy runs through the whole story.
The cast, including Guinevere van Seenus, Remington Williams, Karen Elson, and Natasa Vojnovic, constantly transform. One moment the looks are clean and sculptural, the next more exaggerated or playful. The erased brows and strong eye shapes by Erin Parsons reference Mina’s signature beauty, but feel more modern and experimental here.
Styling by Patti Wilson mixes classic couture with unexpected elements. Ruffles, bold silhouettes, and unusual proportions create a balance between past and future. Hair by Luigi Murenu adds to that transformation, sometimes subtle, sometimes more dramatic, like the bearded look that still feels surprisingly natural.
The more you look at it, the more it starts to make sense. It’s less about one clear message and more about capturing the mindset of an artist who was always pushing things further.
Credits:
Photography: Luigi & Iango
Styling: Patti Wilson
Makeup: Erin Parsons
Hair: Luigi Murenu
Models: Guinevere van Seenus, Remington Williams, Karen Elson, Natasa Vojnovic
Magazine: Vogue Italia, October 2018

posts.caption:
Why did Matthieu Blazy send newsprint down the Chanel Resort ‘27 runway? Who was he referencing and why? Was it an elbow in the rib to John Galliano, the former Christian Dior creative director who has an outsized claim to newsprint fashion?
Contrary to popular belief, Galliano didn’t show newsprint in his infamous ‘hobo chic’ Christian Dior Spring/Summer 2000 collection. It was the ‘Super Fly Girls’ Fall/Winter 2000 show which featured the ‘The Christian Dior Daily’ textile that we all know and love. Needless to say, that print gained cult-fame after Sarah Jessica Parker wore it as Carrie Bradshaw on ‘Sex and the City’ later that year. And that’s on fashion herstory. No, but seriously, it was huge.
Galliano, though, was not the first to do newsprint. Elsa Schiaparelli’s 1935 newsprint, based on actual newspaper clippings of her accomplishments, is the most well-documented early iteration. In her autobiography ‘Shocking Life,’ she wrote about a seaside experience where she saw fishermen’s wives wearing newspapers “twisted into queer shapes of hats.” Upon returning to Paris, she designed the print, commissioned it, and sold it as “blouses, scarves, hats, and all kinds of bathing nonsense.”
It’s sort of curious to clock newsprint in Chanel’s Resort ‘27 collection, which paid homage to Coco Chanel by taking place in Biarritz, the coastal city where she opened her first couture house. It’s no secret that Chanel famously maintained a rivalry with Schiaparelli throughout her career, calling her “that Italian artist who makes clothes.” A 1940’s Vogue editor, Bettina Ballard, once wrote of a ball where Schiaparelli, who had arrived dressed as a surrealist tree, agreed to dance with Chanel who innocently danced her right into a candelabra, setting her costume ablaze.
So, was Blazy extending a long overdue olive branch from Chanel to Schiaparelli? Or was it a taunt? Is there tea on a Blazy/Roseberry friendship of some sort? Either way, the feud between Chanel and Schiaparelli looked to be the elephant in the room (or, on the beach?) at Chanel Resort ‘27.
And speaking of legendary feuds, is it finally time for Ryan Murphy to take on Schiap and Coco?

posts.caption:
Jennifer Rauchet, Pete Hegseth’s wife, has been accused of wearing Temu to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.
Rauchet’s husband Hegseth knows how to spend money with reckless abandon. His career is marred with scandal, much of which alleges financial mismanagement. An analysis published in March revealed $93.4 billion dollars was spent by the DoD in September alone. $9 million of that total was reportedly spent on “Alaskan king crab and lobster tails.” Additional purchases include almost $100k for a grand piano and $139k for donuts. Hegseth has also been accused of attempting to personally profit off the war he set in motion, has faced financial mismanagement accusations prior to becoming Trump’s Secretary of War, and paid off an undisclosed sex assault victim with $50k in 2017.
But it appears that despite Hegseth’s ability to throw money around, it has not yet occurred to him to set any money aside for his wife’s wardrobe budget, leaving her to scrape for the cheapest available options from overseas fast fashion retailers.
Indeed, the petal pink evening dress that Rauchet wore to the WHCD perfectly matches with a product listed on both Temu and Shein. Styled with the dress, Rauchet wore Steve Madden ‘Kenley’ rhinestone heeled sandals and she carried a floral appliqué bag which can be found on Amazon.
It doesn’t look like this is the first time Rauchet has done this: In November of last year, she attended a state dinner in a gown that matches a product listing on Shein. And, the pink dress she wore to her husband’s swearing in ceremony also perfectly matches a product made by Shein’s brand Motf. Choosing an inexpensive option to conform to a family budget is one thing, but is it not a little ironic to wear outfits imported from overseas while being married to the ‘America First’ Secretary of Defense? And while attending important events, no less?

posts.caption:
Predator turned born-again British MAGA stan Russell Brand is under fire (again) after casually admitting on Megyn Kelly’s podcast that he slept with a 16-year-old.
“The plain fact of it is that in Europe and in the United Kingdom, where I’m from, the age of consent is 16, and I did sleep with a 16-year-old when I was 30,” he said. “But when I was 30, I was a very different person. I was a lot younger, and I was an immature 30-year-old.” 🤮
Kelly also raised new sexual assault allegations involving his ex-wife Katy Perry by Ruby Rose, which the singer has denied. “This is probably the old school man in me... I don’t even hear the crime there.”
Brand, 50, is currently facing seven rape and sexual assault charges involving seven women tied to alleged incidents between 1999 and 2009. He has denied all charges.
Meanwhile, he’s spent recent years seemingly aiming for a redemption arc by speaking at multiple Turning Point USA events, cozying up to MAGAts like Dr. Oz and Worm Brain Jr., even receiving a “Global Defender of Freedom” award at Mar-a-Lago last year.
Nothing says moral rebirth quite like rebranding before trial, which begins in October.
#news #trial #threads #reels #fyp

posts.caption:
“Who is the Bleu de Chanel man?” Chanel asked yesterday on IG, as a silhouetted figure of their new fragrance ambassador appeared on their feed.
Well, it definitely wasn’t Timothée Chalamet, who took on the Bleu de Chanel ambassador role in 2023 after the fragrance’s first ever ambassador, Gaspard Ulliel. Ulliel had held the role for twelve years from Bleu de Chanel’s release in 2010 to his tragic death in 2022.
This morning, it was confirmed that Jacob Elordi would replace Chalamet. The announcement features various shots of the actor in profile, highlighting his famously sharp jawline and shaggy mullet. The clip ends on a shot of him pointing straight into the camera with two fingers. In other words, it’s highly masculine, authoritative, and a bit playful in contrast to the minimalistic and soft announcement for Chalamet just a few years ago.
It makes sense: Elordi has built an illustrious reputation that transcends his breakout TV role in Euphoria. He has an Oscar nom under his belt, has starred alongside Margot Robbie, who is also a Chanel ambassador and was featured with Elordi in an ad for Chanel No. 5 last year. Chanel describes him as “a deeply determined man, driven by his instinct.”
For reference, Chalamet’s announcement in 2023 was much less specific, stating only that Bleu was “a fragrance for the man who is deeply himself.” Perhaps that was Chalamet’s selling point at the time. Then again, perhaps that was Bleu de Chanel’s selling point at the time. What changed?
Since Chalamet’s appointment at Chanel, he graduated from the boyish, French-ish heartthrob with the pretty loose curls and the gender-bending outfits, becoming instead a devil-may-care troublemaker with a taste for hype styling and awkward facial hair. And, the charm that made him beloved in his early career all but wore off completely when he made incendiary comments about opera and ballet last February. Chanel, one Threads user notes, is a “major patron” of the Paris Opera Ballet. Could these be related?
Further, will two or three stints be the norm for Chanel’s fragrance ambassadorships? Or can Ulliel’s longstanding role be repeated with Elordi?
In any case, Congrats Jacob!

posts.caption:
Babe, wake up! Ranveer has designed his own adidas Originals Superstar. Superstar for THEE superstar!!
A Ranveer Singh for adidas Originals sneaker feels like a long time coming. The RGB colour-way track-sets, the bucket hats, the special collabs, and the plethora of shoes. Ranveer’s wardrobe choice and the Originals identity have merged over time to create a streetwear aesthetic that is instantly recognisable. He has sported a style that no one in his level of fame dares to tread, and the brand has served as a cultural lightning rod, bringing to him the best of global streetwear.
So finally when he gets to be a part of adidas India’s first ever statement shoe drop, it seems like a no-brainer. A MAJOR choice if you may. To collab at this level with Originals is not just an acknowledgment of Ranveer’s love for the brand, but also of his influence. Not influencer, baby, INFLUENTIAL. His ‘superstar’-dom, if you may. As they say, strike the iron while it’s hot, and adidas Originals has clearly clocked it, because if anyone can sell a shoe right now, it’s HIM.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There’s no forced “full circle” narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation — the same artist, only more settled into himself. “I don’t feel like I’m at such a different place in my career,” he says. “I feel like I’m at a different place and point as a human; I’m developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.” That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the 🔗in bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

posts.caption:
Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@matthieu_blazy represents the new guard at @chanelofficial, yes—but for his debut resort Tuesday afternoon, he returned to old territory. In the seaside town of Biarritz, he presented the house’s resort 2027 collection at the Le Casino Municipal, just steps from where Gabrielle Chanel established her first couture house in 1915.
As expected, many of the signatures Blazy has established thus far at Chanel—louche pantsuits, simple Basque stripe jumpers styled with ball skirts, his now-famous It shoes—were present, albeit with an unmistakable seaside current through it all. Accessories continued to be a highlight, while the maison’s signature CC logo cheekily popped up throughout the collection—less a branding exercise, and more architectural in its deployment across garments.
“Two years ago I came to Biarritz,” Blazy said backstage following the show. “I was going to the lighthouse, there was this extraordinary mural depicting two mermaids and a boat. For me it was a wonderful starting point.”
Tap the link in bio to read @matt.velasco’s full review.

posts.caption:
@billieeilish is still finding new dimensions.
For one, she tells ELLE she’s eyeing some acting opportunities. In March, it was reported that Eilish was in talks to make her film acting debut in an upcoming adaptation of Sylvia Plath’s 1963 novel ‘The Bell Jar.’ Though the role itself is yet to be announced, Oscar-winning director Sarah Polley will be at the helm. Might she direct a film of her own one day? That’s up in the air, but according to director James Cameron, the most heroic role Eilish could play—at least for the moment—is herself.
Head to the link in bio to read ELLE’s full May 2026 cover story with the Grammy-winning artist.

posts.caption:
For as long as she’s been in the public eye, @billieeilish has faced countless discussions about her fashion choices—and her body.
“I’m not the first person who’s worn baggy clothes,” she says of her wardrobe, citing inspirations like Harlem fashion influencer Bloody Osiris (@bloodyosiris) and hip-hop icons Tyler, the Creator (@feliciathegoat) and Missy Elliott (@missymisdemeanorelliott). She notes the freedom of movement afforded by these clothes, and the resulting gender euphoria. “I had a really, really toxic relationship with my body,” Eilish tells ELLE. ”I didn’t want to be seen as feminine, and therefore weak. It’s not right. I’ve found a good way of not feeling like that.”
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish contemplates her newfound relationship with touring, her brother, her body, and more. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Together, @billieeilish and her brother, @finneas, are a multidisciplinary pop powerhouse. But for her last tour, she performed almost all of it without him onstage with her.
“It was a few years in the making,” says Eilish of her decision to tour without her brother. Although fans have speculated about the nature of his absence, the siblings tell ELLE that her touring solo was a mutual decision. “It’s basically true that I don’t like touring, but I love the show part of it,” says Finneas. “And I love being around Billie. This past year, when she would be on tour for months, I missed her a lot.”
He even dropped by a few of Eilish’s shows for moral support—and eventually joined her onstage to sing. “If I never saw Finneas at all, I might literally never make a song again,” Eilish tells ELLE.
For ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about how their relationship as siblings and creative partners has evolved, the process of taking her latest tour from the stage to the screen with James Cameron, and her foray into acting. Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@billieeilish’s fierce rebel spirit has long permeated her career, particularly when speaking up about injustice.
Upon accepting her latest Grammy Award in February, she riled up conservative politicians and commentators when she spoke out against the escalating ICE raids, stating, “No one is illegal on stolen land.” And when she appeared at the WSJ. Magazine Innovator Awards in October, she openly challenged billionaires in the room to part with some of their wealth—and her speech wasn’t just lip service. That same night, she pledged $11.5 million of the revenue from her last tour to multiple charities and organizations. “I was raised like this,” she tells ELLE.
Despite her critics’ best efforts to tamp down her fighting spirit, what keeps her coming back to the public eye is the camaraderie she shares with her fans through her songwriting. “What I am going through actually isn’t just me, it’s everyone,” she says. “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.”
Read ELLE’s full May 2026 cover story featuring the “Wildflower” singer at the link in bio.

posts.caption:

Now I’m trying to take it all to heart as I peruse my next collection
posts.alt_text: Photo by Fashion is Psychology™️ on March 19, 2025.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠

Not all sneakers have the same values 👟👀⁠

In fact, most of the biggest brands rate quite middling, according to our analysis: Nike and Adidas rate “It’s a Start” while brands like Asics and Golden Goose score “Not Good Enough”. This isn’t surprising, considering these are the biggest brands in the world—which largely correlates with worse values 🚮⁠
⁠
You do have options, if and when you’re looking for sneakers. From innovative vegan brands to fair trade advocates, there are shoes that align with your values. posts.caption:
Garment workers need our help by advocating further for change in fashion 💔⁠
⁠
For many workers throughout fashion’s supply chain, there is no guarantee of safe working conditions, proper payment, or benefits, all of which takes advantage of often vulnerable people as the biggest brand seek profits at the expense of persons they continue to take advantage of 😠⁠
⁠
A key way workers can create change is through collective bargaining, which allows workers and their employers to negotiate around their conditions. This is a fundamental element of autonomy—but not all brands are working to ensure this is the case, as many enable a problematic status quo.⁠
⁠
🚨 This is where you come in: by speaking up to brands and local governments, maintaining pressure to change the system, we can ensure workers worldwide are getting their due. Swipe to learn about this problem and how you can help. Explore the full story at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #SHEINGals #Zara #TemuFinds #SustainableFashion⁠

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption: Between resale alerts, proxy services, and hush-hush WhatsApp groups, a new kind of consumer has emerged: the supershopper. For this ultra-determined breed, tracking down rare pieces from labels like @Chanelofficial and @Prada—whether new or vintage—is less a hobby and more an obsession. Supershoppers are like Olympic athletes, but they play the long game in pursuit of fashion’s most elusive finds. In Bazaar’s April 2026 issue, we spoke with some of our favorite supershoppers to uncover their secrets for scoring the most coveted designer pieces.

posts.caption: I was dubious about the wisdom of this visit, but King Charles's speech is a masterclass in one-up-manship without being offensive; stating the facts when others choose to deny history; and highlighting the great and the best facets of our relationship with the US as its founding partner. posts.caption:
Matthieu Blazy’s latest show proved the hype around him isn’t fading anytime soon. Showing where Gabrielle Chanel once worked, he mixed classic @chanelofficial vibes with a more relaxed, modern feel, think pink denim suits, sporty layers, bold logos, and a diverse cast including older models and a pregnant runway moment. His now-signature €3,100 jeans are becoming a symbol of this new direction, blending luxury with a more easygoing attitude, while pieces like a shimmering mermaid-style gown and newspaper-print suits nodded to Chanel’s history with a playful twist.⁠
⁠
Off the runway, it was just as much a spectacle. Chanel basically took over the city with parties and big-name guests, showing how these destination shows have become fashion’s version of blockbuster events. With ultra-wealthy clients spending big and rival brands planning their own global shows, Chanel is focused on staying desirable long-term, not rushing growth, and keeping all eyes firmly on them.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 28, 2026. May be an image of leather, magazine, purse, clutch and text that says 'MATTHIEU BLAZY'S CHANEL CRUISE 2026/27 BAGS ARE ANYTHING BORING H'.

posts.caption:
@jaz_jaz_jaz__ answered that.⁠
⁠
The original frames are deconstructed and reshaped, wrapped in leather with soft, pochette-inspired volumes backed by felt. It still has all the original chair DNA but looks a lot more sophisticated and usable. ⁠
⁠
Structured yet fluid, it moves easily between a shoulder bag and a briefcase, carrying a bit of the past into the present.

posts.caption:
Long defined by iconic ’90s kits and Britpop nostalgia, the century-old sportswear label has quietly evolved into a sharp operator in modern streetwear. Its latest move, a collaboration with niche screen-printing project Secondhalf.pdf, shows just how tuned-in it is: instead of chasing mass hype, @umbro is spotlighting underground creators who remix football culture into artful, offbeat pieces. This shift marks a clear departure from its 2000s lull, when lost kit deals pushed it toward safer, nostalgia-driven partnerships.⁠
⁠
Now, Umbro’s resurgence is fueled by smart, culture-first collaborations. Alongside heavyweights like Supreme, Palace, and Slam Jam, it taps emerging designers and subcultural innovators such as JW Customs, LC23, and Adam Jones. The result is a brand that feels genuinely current rather than retro-reliant, proving that in streetwear, relevance comes down to one thing: who you choose to stand next to.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 28, 2026. May be an image of sweater, sportswear, sports equipment, ball and text.

posts.caption:
@asaprocky steps into a new lane with his first optical drop for @rayban, the Metal Collection.⁠
⁠
Built around sleek wire frames, tinted lenses, and just enough attitude, the range feels less like a debut and more like a natural extension of Rocky’s long-running grip on style culture. The collection is polished and has a retro feel without feeling costume-ish. The frames are somewhat reminiscent of vintage Cartier and Jean Paul Gaultier styles. ⁠
⁠
With prices ranging from $200 to $250 USD, you can now shop the entire selection on Ray-Ban.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 27, 2026 tagging @rayban. May be an image of magazine, sunglasses and text that says 'RAY-BAN ENTERS NEW PHASE WITH A$AP ROCKY'S METAL COLLECTION H'.

posts.caption:
@eddiebauer is chasing a very Japanese kind of glow-up.⁠
⁠
Since relaunching in Japan, the legacy American outfitter has started to mirror the playbook that made The North Face Japan so culturally sharp: heritage gear, locally reinterpreted, and elevated through a premium diffusion line. Enter Eddie Bauer Black Label and a key creative twist, HYKE, the quietly influential Japanese brand that previously refined The North Face’s technical wear into understated, fashion-forward staples. Now, with that partnership shifted over, HYKE is giving Eddie Bauer’s classics new life, from oversized takes on the iconic Skyliner jacket to reworked MA-1 puffers and clean, logo-light everyday pieces.⁠
⁠
The formula feels familiar but promising. HYKE’s touch leans on subtle tailoring, relaxed silhouettes, and restraint rather than flashy reinvention, turning rugged Americana into city-ready uniforms. If The North Face Purple Label proved this hybrid can thrive, Eddie Bauer Japan is now following the same trail, with early results suggesting it might just carve out its own lane in the “quiet outdoor” movement.

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Some people dress for the season. @avneetkaur_13 dresses for herself and somehow that always ends up being more relevant than whatever the moment is calling for. 💖
That’s the thing about @avneetkaur_13 . The confidence isn’t loud. It doesn’t need to be. It just sits there, in every frame, making a quiet and very convincing case for doing things entirely your own way.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , Avneet Kaur makes monochromatic indigo feel like an entire coastal state of mind. Soft silhouettes, printed textures, street-style cool — this is warm-weather dressing that carries zero noise and all the impact. ✨
Every look lands the way she does: effortlessly, intentionally, and with just enough quiet confidence to make you rethink everything hanging in your wardrobe.

posts.caption:
@matthieu_blazy is taking @chanelofficial back to its roots 🇫🇷 For the house’s Cruise 2027 collection, Blazy is touching down in Biarritz—the exact coastal city where Gabrielle Chanel set up her first couture shop back in 1915. Channeling that original era of relaxed, sporty elegance, the double C’s are officially taking over the Basque coast once again 🌊
Credit: Joyce Li/Hypebeast
posts.alt_text:
@dontoliver always knew a number one record was in the cards. “It kind of felt inevitable,” the musician told Hypebeast. It took five studio albums to get here, but ‘Octane’ proved itself to be the instant-classic he always believed he was destined to make. But despite the whirlwind success, he’s thinking about the long road ahead.

“I create music through an old-school lens. Marvin Gaye, Prince, all those guys made classic records that I still listen to today, and I want that same type of longevity.”

In a wide-ranging interview, Toliver lays out his plans for what’s next: “You can’t take your hands off the wheel. You’ve got to understand the responsibility.”

Check out the full Hypebeast Magazine cover story via the link in bio. Available now for order on @hbx.

posts.caption:
@dontoliver always knew a number one record was in the cards. “It kind of felt inevitable,” the musician told Hypebeast. It took five studio albums to get here, but ‘Octane’ proved itself to be the instant-classic he always believed he was destined to make. But despite the whirlwind success, he’s thinking about the long road ahead.
“I create music through an old-school lens. Marvin Gaye, Prince, all those guys made classic records that I still listen to today, and I want that same type of longevity.”
In a wide-ranging interview, Toliver lays out his plans for what’s next: “You can’t take your hands off the wheel. You’ve got to understand the responsibility.”
Check out the full Hypebeast Magazine cover story via the link in bio. Available now for order on @hbx.

posts.caption:
@mm6maisonmargiela and @salomon aren’t done revisiting the XT-MM6, but they’re ready to introduce their next collaborative model: the CROSS DUST ⁠
⁠
This new take on Salomon’s trail-running Cross silhouette features a technical gaiter inspired by Maison Margiela’s signature dust bags and neck tag graphics. MM6 and Salomon’s cobranded gaiter snugly encases the body of the shoe, hiding the bungee cord laces underneath for a streamlined look.⁠
⁠
For the XT-MM6, new colorways include an ’80s-inspired lime yellow, along with a range of cool grays and muted yellow accents. An all-black colorway is offered for both the XT-MM6 and the CROSS DUST, completing the range with a stealthy variant. Finally, the XT-15 Backpack returns with improved comfort and ergonomics, and a rain cover. ⁠
⁠
The MM6 × Salomon SS26 collection will be available on April 29, 2026, at MM6 and Salomon e-commerce platforms and shops, as well as select multibrand retailers.⁠
⁠
Photo: Salomon x MM6 Maison Margiela⁠

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:

We peek into his world for our April cover, and it’s a garden of blessings. Read the cover story in the link in the bio.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Le borse di @Matthieu_Blazy per @ChanelOfficial Cruise 2026/27 sono un intero vocabolario a sé. Non c’è un solo mood — c’è tutto: la tote in paglia intrecciata a righe multicolor con logo CC per chi arriva in spiaggia con stile, il duffle bag bicolor in caviar e suede per chi parte senza checkin, la flap in camoscio celeste baby per chi preferisce non scegliere tra casual e couture. E poi i pezzi che fermano il respiro: la classica flap ricamata a mano con pesci e alghe marine — un tableau vivant da portare a tracolla — e la minaudière in tweed pied-de-poule che sembra uscita da una maison di cappelleria d’antan. Blazy non ha ridisegnato la borsa Chanel. L’ha raccontata da capo.
Ph: Getty Images
#ChanelCruise #Chanel #Biarritz #MatthieuBlazy #ChanelB

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Planes, trains, and automobiles—check, check, and check. @Chanelofficial has staged them all.⁠
⁠
Ahead of @Matthieu_Blazy’s first Chanel Cruise collection in Biarritz tomorrow, we revisit some of the maison’s most unforgettable runways because no one does runways quite like Chanel. From Coco Beach Spring/Summer 2019, where the Grand Palais transformed into a shoreline, to the Spring/Summer 2016 Air Chanel show complete with a branded jet, spectacle has long been part of the house vocabulary.⁠
⁠
When Karl Lagerfeld arrived at Chanel in 1983, he didn’t just reshape the label, he redefined what a fashion show could be. Through Virginie Viard and now Matthieu Blazy, that spirit of transformation endures, each continuing Chanel’s legacy of world-building on the runway. Blazy has already extended that language through immersive sets, from ethereal galaxies to enchanted forests and surreal urban environments.⁠
⁠
Ahead of tomorrow’s Cruise show, revisit Chanel’s most extravagant runways at the link in bio.⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images

posts.caption:
⁠This year’s Met Gala dress code reads simply: “Fashion is Art.” And yet, what does that actually mean?⁠
⁠
The overarching theme of the Met Gala Costume Institute exhibition, “Costume Art,” turns to the body—examining how fashion shapes, frames, and reimagines it. With that in mind, we return to the L’OFFICIEL archive, sourcing early works by Jean Paul Gaultier and Thierry Mugler, among others—designers who long treated the body as canvas, illusion, and spectacle—as enduring points of reference for the night ahead.⁠
⁠
Photos courtesy of the L'OFFICIEL Archive
#MetGala #MetGalaFashion #MetGala2026

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
The Mercedes‑Benz G‑Class, widely known as the G-Wagon, began as a rugged military project before evolving into one of the most recognizable luxury SUVs in the world. Its development started in 1973, when the Shah of Iran requested a durable off-road vehicle, leading Mercedes‑Benz to partner with Steyr‑Daimler‑Puch to create a highly capable all-terrain vehicle. The production version, called the Geländewagen, officially launched in 1979, built with a ladder-frame chassis, locking differentials, and strong off-road capability.
Through the 1980s and 1990s, the G-Class gradually transitioned from a purely utilitarian vehicle to a premium lifestyle SUV. Mercedes introduced features such as leather interiors, advanced safety systems like ABS, and improved comfort elements, positioning the G-Wagon as a rare blend of durability and luxury. Its reputation for reliability was reinforced by real-world endurance, including long-distance expeditions that showcased the vehicle’s ability to perform in extreme conditions.
Despite major technological upgrades over the decades, the G-Class has retained its signature boxy silhouette, flat panels, and upright stance, making it instantly recognizable across generations. Modern versions combine that iconic design with powerful engines, luxury-focused cabins, and advanced driver assistance systems, transforming what began as a military-inspired machine into a global symbol of performance, heritage, and status.
#Luxurious #MercedesBenz

Instead of the usual golfer figurine, they have stripped it all the way back to the motion itself, turning the arc of a perfect golf swing into a sleek, sculptural form.
This is what happens when a luxury car brand brings its design language to sport. Does a trophy need to look traditional to feel prestigious?

posts.caption:
The Mini Cooper S Convertible JCW Pack has officially arrived in India, bringing a sportier edge to the brand’s open-top lineup with a price tag of ₹61.50 lakh (ex-showroom). Offered as a completely built unit in limited numbers, the model adds an extra layer of exclusivity, with bookings already underway and deliveries beginning soon.
This new variant builds on the standard Cooper S Convertible but introduces the John Cooper Works (JCW) styling package, giving the car a more aggressive visual identity. Updates include new colour options such as Midnight Black and Legend Grey, gloss-black exterior elements, redesigned front and rear bumpers, bonnet stripes on select shades, 17-inch black alloy wheels, and JCW sport brakes. A black soft-top roof completes the sharper, performance-inspired look.
The convertible roof remains one of the model’s standout highlights. Its electric soft top can open in just 18 seconds and can also function like a sunroof, even while driving at speeds of up to 30 km/h. Inside, the cabin features JCW sports seats, a JCW steering wheel with paddle shifters, a 9.4-inch circular OLED touchscreen, head-up display, Harman Kardon sound system, wireless charging, and connected tech including Apple CarPlay and Android Auto.
Under the hood, the car continues with a 2.0-litre turbo-petrol engine producing around 204 hp and 300 Nm of torque, paired with a 7-speed dual-clutch automatic transmission. It accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in approximately 6.9 seconds and reaches a top speed of about 237 km/h, delivering performance that matches its sporty design.
With its limited availability, performance credentials, and distinctive styling upgrades, the new JCW Pack version positions the Mini convertible as a niche yet highly desirable choice for buyers seeking an open-top driving experience with added attitude.
Images: @miniindia_official
#Luxurious #MiniCooper

posts.caption:
Diljit Dosanjh made a striking appearance on The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon, turning heads not only with his performance but also with a standout fashion moment in Chanel designed under creative direction of Matthieu Blazy.
For the show, Diljit wore a sharply tailored black co-ord ensemble that blended classic Chanel elegance with a modern edge, reinforcing his growing reputation as a global style figure. The polished look quickly caught attention online, adding to the buzz already surrounding his appearance.
Beyond fashion, the highlight of the episode was his lively cultural exchange with host Jimmy Fallon. Diljit taught Fallon signature bhangra moves, creating a playful moment that went viral across social media. He also performed his track “Morni” and promoted his album AURA, bringing Punjabi music and dance to a massive international audience.
During the conversation, Diljit also reflected on the historic Komagata Maru incident, drawing a powerful contrast between past discrimination faced by Indians abroad and the present-day global recognition of Punjabi culture. His message added depth to an episode that combined entertainment, fashion, and cultural storytelling on a global stage.
Images: @diljitdosanjh @fallontonightbts @fallontonight
Photographer: @toddowyoung
#Luxurious #Chanel

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy’s (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
“It wasn’t like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,” says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings — and one very intentional gym switch — finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
“I realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,” she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don’t need grand gestures to feel certain.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise — only truth.
“A few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,” she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full story from the link in bio.

Think breezy lehengas, easy suits, a brocade corset and skirt set, a summer dress for the honeymoon that still feels light, and pieces you’ll actually wear again, right down to kolhapuris that don’t try too hard.

Actor Mehreen Pirzada (@mehreenpirzadaa) and Arsh Aulakh (@iamarshaulakh) just gave us the template for a summer wedding of dreams.
Announced with a simple “26.04.26”, the ceremony unfolded in Himachal Pradesh, with a soft, pastel pink palette setting the tone.
Mehreen’s embroidered anarkali, paired with minimal makeup and statement jewellery, felt effortless yet considered, while Arsh mirrored the mood in a coordinated look.
A reminder that summer weddings do not need excess. Sometimes, all it takes is the right colour, the right setting, and a sense of ease.

On Thursday night in Manhattan, stars like Ethan Hawke and Noah Wylie mingled with Dakota Johnson, Jennie and Hilary Duff at the Time 100 Gala. The annual list highlights the most important people of the year, from stage and screen to activism and politics. This year’s list includes President Trump, Pope Leo XIV and the YouTuber MrBeast. Claire Danes sipped martinis with Alan Cumming, Victoria Beckham caught up with Zoe Saldaña and Natasha Lyonne and Anderson .Paak whispered the night away.

posts.caption:
The recent cover featuring Anna Wintour and Meryl Streep is being widely celebrated as proof that the fashion industry has finally “embraced older women.” The reality is probably less romantic.
There is, of course, a cultural shift underway. Questions of visibility, representation, and age are far more present today than they were a decade or two ago. Seeing women in their 60s on a Vogue cover would have been almost unthinkable in the past.
At the same time, fashion remains a business. And the reality is that the most powerful consumers in the luxury segment are often not in their 20s, but in their 40s, 50s, and beyond. They have the income, the assets (and often a greater willingness) to spend on quality, status, or personal reward. In Europe and the United States in particular, the core of luxury consumption already skews toward these age groups.
From that perspective, the cover feels less like a radical cultural breakthrough and more like a smart alignment with the realities of its audience.
That doesn’t diminish its symbolic value, visibility matters. But it does remind us that progress in fashion is rarely driven by idealism alone. More often than not, it also follows purchasing power.
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 24, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, people standing, magazine, turtleneck and text that says 'Why Fashion Suddenly Loves Older Women 魚'.

But you can head off a lot of the stress by sitting down with your partner early on and coming up with a clear vision of what you want. Identifying your priorities, desires and budget before diving into the details will simplify the process and keep you on track. To help you navigate these decisions, the experts at The New York Times Wedding section created a quiz to determine what kind of wedding is the best fit for you. posts.caption:
At a starry premiere for "The Devil Wears Prada 2," fashion industry figures reflected on just how much the original film got right.
The movie, released in 2006, starred Anne Hathaway as the blunder-prone assistant to Miranda Priestly, a fashion magazine editor played by Meryl Streep who has a gaze withering enough to penetrate the darkest of sunglasses. Now, the sequel considers how their dynamic — and the media landscape they occupy — has shifted after two decades.
At the film’s premiere in Manhattan on Monday night, Hathaway and Streep posed with their co-stars Stanley Tucci and Emily Blunt on a red carpet outside Lincoln Center. Also present were the real-life inspirations, Vogue's Anna Wintour and the head of editorial content for American Vogue, Chloe Malle.
Inside David Geffen Hall, industry figures reflected on how much the movie had in common with the world it portrays. Tap the link in our bio for highlights from the premiere. Visuals by @apisukh

The runway was staged like her Upper East Side apartment, and the clothes reflected Charlotte’s signature aesthetic of polka dots, florals, gingham, and tailoring. The opening and most iconic look was a cream dress called “the Lily dress,” referencing a scene from the second movie in which Charlotte’s vintage #Valentino skirt is ruined by her daughter. #MarkGong reimagined the skirt turning the paint marks into hand beaded handprint details.

posts.caption:
Tomorrow will mark #MatthieuBlazy’s first cruise show for #Chanel in the beachside town of Biarritz, a place of great significance to #CocoChanel. With all the buzz around it, i can’t help but think about the infamous spring 2019 beach show.
#KarlLagerfeld covered the Grand Palais in real sand (sourced from a quarry outside Paris which was returned after the show) and a functioning shoreline. The set was completed with a wooden boardwalk, a lifeguard tower, and beach huts. Models walked barefoot through the sand, making the whole thing feel more like a leisurely stroll along the beach than a fashion show.
(Images courtesy of Chanel / Getty / Collector Square / Kim Weston Arnold / Marcus Tondo / Vogue Runway)
